hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Semi-detached (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value an early-twentieth-century vernacular-style House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their shared roofline and segmentally arched window openings at the second storey. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way WB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks & roadway | E-W commercial connector

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their shared roofline and segmentally arched window openings at the second storey. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century.

facade_description: Two-storey early-twentieth-century converted semi-detached house-form with a 5.2-metre mixed-material frontage and gable roof. Built 1904-1913 as a pair with 147 Baldwin, sharing a roofline and segmentally arched second-storey window openings. The ground floor has been converted to commercial use (The Jerk Spot) with a storefront insertion. Two double-hung sash windows on the upper storey. No direct photo observations are available; description based on database records.
